activities.title: The psychology of payment methods
activities.content:
**How you pay changes how much you spend.** This is one of the most well-researched findings in behavioural economics.

Studies consistently show that people spend **12–18% more** when using cards or digital payments compared to cash. Why? Cash is physical and feels real — spending $50 cash feels like a loss. Tapping a card takes milliseconds and feels abstract.

In New Zealand, 85% of transactions are now cashless. This convenience comes with a hidden cost: it is much easier to overspend without realising it.
activities.notePrompt: Do you think you spend more when paying by card vs cash? Why or why not?

activities.title: Eftpos and debit cards
activities.content:
**Eftpos** is New Zealand's debit payment network — you pay directly from your bank account.

**Advantages:**
- Convenient and widely accepted
- No interest (it is your own money)
- Easy to track via banking apps
- Contactless tap and go up to $200

**Disadvantages:**
- Easy to overspend — no friction
- Impulse purchases happen more readily
- Overdraft fees if you go negative
- Peer pressure to spend at group events

Eftpos is fine for daily spending — the key is checking your balance before spending, not after.
activities.notePrompt: How often do you check your bank balance before making a purchase?

activities.title: Credit cards — a double-edged tool
activities.content:
Credit cards let you borrow money for purchases and repay later. In NZ, major options include Visa, Mastercard, and Amex.

**Potential benefits (if paid in full each month):**
- Purchase protection and dispute resolution
- Rewards points / cashback
- Interest-free period (usually 44–55 days)
- Emergency spending buffer

**The danger:** Average NZ credit card interest rate = **22% per annum**. If you carry a balance:
- $1,000 balance = $220/year in interest
- Minimum payments only = could take 10+ years to pay off

**Rule:** Only use credit cards if you will pay the full balance every month. Otherwise, do not get one.
activities.notePrompt: Do you think you would be disciplined enough to pay a credit card balance in full every month? Be honest.

questions.question: Sarah has a credit card balance of $2,400 at 22% annual interest. She only makes minimum payments of $48/month. Approximately how much interest will she pay in the first year?
questions.explanation: 22% of $2,400 = $528 in interest for the first year — more than 10 times her monthly payment. The balance barely reduces. This is why credit card debt is so dangerous.

questions.question: What is the main psychological reason people spend more with cards than cash?
options: Card payments feel less like real money
questions.explanation: Physical cash feels real — losing it stings. Card or phone payments are abstract and instant, reducing the emotional resistance to spending. This is well-documented in behavioural economics research.

activities.title: Buy Now Pay Later — understanding Afterpay
activities.content:
**BNPL (Buy Now Pay Later)** services like Afterpay, Laybuy, and Humm let you split purchases into instalments — usually interest-free.

**How Afterpay works:**
- Split purchase into 4 payments every 2 weeks
- No interest if paid on time
- Late fee: $10 per missed payment (capped)

**The real risk:**
- Makes expensive items feel affordable (a $200 item becomes 'just $50 today')
- Easy to have multiple Afterpay purchases running simultaneously
- If you miss payments, fees compound
- Reported to credit agencies — can affect future loan applications

**NZ statistic:** 67% of Afterpay users admit to buying things they would not have bought if they had to pay upfront.
activities.notePrompt: Have you used Afterpay or similar? Did it change what you bought or how much you spent?

questions.statement: Using a credit card always leads to debt.
questions.explanation: Credit cards can be used responsibly if the full balance is paid every month within the interest-free period. Used this way, you get the benefits (protection, rewards) without paying interest.

questions.statement: BNPL services like Afterpay are always free to use.
questions.explanation: Afterpay is free IF you pay on time. Miss a payment and you pay $10 immediately, plus another $7 if still unpaid 7 days later. Multiple missed payments add up quickly.

questions.statement: Spending with physical cash tends to make people more conscious of how much they are spending.
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows that parting with physical notes creates stronger emotional responses than digital payments, leading to more considered spending decisions.

activities.instruction: Classify each payment scenario as Low Risk or High Risk

items.text: Paying rent by automatic bank transfer
Using Afterpay for $400 shoes you don't need
Using a credit card and paying the full balance monthly
Using credit card for groceries and carrying the balance
Weekly grocery shop on debit card with budget tracked
Tap-and-go for everything with no balance checks
Cash envelope system for discretionary spending
